bins.name: Assert FERC jurisdiction clearly
bins.desc: Eolian argues FERC has ample authority over large load interconnection under the FPA because interconnection is a transmission service. It supports a bright-line voltage and capacity threshold rather than the seven-factor test to avoid protracted litigation and enable a predictable, administrable process. The filing also stresses the need for a clear transition rule to prevent a regulatory gap between state and federal authority.
quotes: the interconnection of large loads to the interstate transmission system is a transmission service in precisely the same way that the interconnection of generators to the transmission system is a transmission service, over which the Commission has long asserted jurisdiction.
the Commission should establish a bright-line standard over which large loads are subject to a federally-regulated interconnection process instead of relying on the seven-factor test.

the Commission should state as clearly as possible in any forthcoming NOPR (or before) that it intends to provide for a transition rule and how it will propose to handle large load interconnections that have begun but not completed an existing state-jurisdictional process at the time the relevant tariff provisions go into effect.
Eolian supports the Commission treading carefully into new jurisdictional areas that have traditionally been regulated by states

bins.name: Bright-line size and voltage threshold
bins.desc: Eolian recommends FERC adopt a straightforward capacity and voltage threshold (such as 20 MW at 60 to 100 kV) to define which large loads are subject to federal interconnection rules. This replaces the ambiguous seven-factor test and eliminates litigation risk that would delay the rulemaking's benefits.
quotes: the Commission should establish a bright-line standard over which large loads are subject to a federally-regulated interconnection process instead of relying on the seven-factor test.

the Commission could establish that the interconnection of large loads (e.g., 20 MW, as proposed by the ANOPR) to facilities with voltage greater than or equal to between 60-100 kV would be subject to federal interconnection standards.

bins.name: Integrated load and generation study
bins.desc: Eolian has directly experienced refusals by grid operators to study load and proximate generation together, resulting in duplicative and avoidable upgrade requirements. It supports a coordinated study process that considers hybrid facility load and generation jointly to minimize network upgrade costs and study timelines.
quotes: Eolian has experienced multiple cases where a transmission provider's refusal to perform an integrated study of load and generation has caused duplicative upgrade requirements that could have been avoided if load and generation had been considered together, which has impacted the business viability of both load and generation.

serial streamlined study of load and hybrid facilities is possible where the facility nominates operating limits (in MW) for aggregate net injections and withdrawals of power, installs necessary control equipment to ensure that the facility is capable of following the dispatch controls of the transmission system operator and balancing authority and respecting operating limits at all times, and is capable at any time of complete self-supply or otherwise curtailing the load such that the facility can take transmission service on a non-firm basis.

bins.name: Hybrid resource rights and definitions
bins.desc: Eolian proposes a broad, technologically neutral definition of hybrid facilities that includes storage and allows for diverse configurations and business models regardless of meter placement or ownership structure. It urges FERC to require non-firm and conditional firm transmission service so hybrid resources can inject and withdraw at their full potential, and to establish enforceable flexibility commitment frameworks for both transmission and capacity needs.
quotes: recognizing enforceable commitments from large load customers and hybrid facilities to curtail and dispatch when called upon, providing transmission service and balancing authority operations protocols that match these commitments, and appropriately rewarding these commitments with preferred treatment in the interconnection process.

the Commission should direct transmission providers to provide for a FERC-jurisdictional load interconnection process that grants queue priority to loads that make commitments to curtail, and hybrid facilities that make commitments to dispatch and curtail, such that (a) the load or hybrid resource reduces the system's need for capacity or Network Resources, and/or (b) avoids the need for network upgrades.

the Commission should direct transmission providers to offer non-firm or conditional firm transmission service to hybrid resources that allow them to inject and withdraw up to their full potential, subject to specified system conditions and their flexibility commitments.
Without access to non-firm or conditional firm service, much of the economic value of the hybrid resource model will be lost.
the Commission should also require that transmission providers allow a pathway to permanent non-firm operation that does not require a subsequent service conversion.

bins.name: Streamlined study timeline
bins.desc: Eolian proposes a serial streamlined study process for qualifying hybrid facilities that can complete in 60 to 90 days per transmission owner by avoiding deliverability analysis where operating limits eliminate the need for network upgrades. This contrasts with the multi-year timelines that currently impede large load interconnection.
quotes: each transmission owner can independently conduct streamlined hybrid interconnection processes (even within an ISO/RTO region) in serial fashion in as little as 60-90 days.

bins.name: Option to build and cost transparency
bins.desc: Eolian supports extending the option to build to large load interconnection customers and urges greater standardization, cost reporting, and auditability to overcome the barriers that currently make this option difficult to access in practice.
quotes: The Commission should build upon its experience in the generator interconnection process by further reducing barriers to use of the option to build.

bins.name: Standardized interconnection study process
bins.desc: Eolian supports adoption of pro forma large load interconnection procedures to eliminate the non-transparent, jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction variation that currently enables undue discrimination. It proposes a serial streamlined study track for hybrid facilities and queue priority for curtailable loads as the two central study reforms.
quotes: Eolian strongly supports the Commission's effort to ensure the timely and orderly interconnection of large loads to the transmission system.

existing interconnection processes for large loads and hybrid resources suffer from a lack of standardization—both because they differ markedly from jurisdiction to jurisdiction, and because they often rely on unwritten, non-transparent practices and afford unwarranted discretion to the local utility.

The absence of standardized, transparent procedures creates opportunities for undue discrimination in queue processing and transmission upgrade cost assignment as public utilities negotiate individual contracts with loads.

the Commission should direct transmission providers to provide for a FERC-jurisdictional load interconnection process that grants queue priority to loads that make commitments to curtail, and hybrid facilities that make commitments to dispatch and curtail, such that (a) the load or hybrid resource reduces the system's need for capacity or Network Resources, and/or (b) avoids the need for network upgrades.

serial streamlined study of load and hybrid facilities is possible where the facility nominates operating limits (in MW) for aggregate net injections and withdrawals of power, installs necessary control equipment to ensure that the facility is capable of following the dispatch controls of the transmission system operator and balancing authority and respecting operating limits at all times, and is capable at any time of complete self-supply or otherwise curtailing the load such that the facility can take transmission service on a non-firm basis.

bins.name: Protect existing customers from cost shift
bins.desc: Eolian argues that the current regime causes existing transmission customers to absorb upgrade costs triggered by large loads, and that enabling flexibility commitments is the primary mechanism to avoid those upgrades and reduce cost shifts. It also supports an expanded and standardized option to build as a tool to lower interconnection costs.
quotes: Flexibility commitments from loads and hybrid resources can help to protect existing customers by avoiding the need for transmission system upgrades and allowing large loads to reduce the resource adequacy impacts of their interconnection and allow large loads—and the resources needed to serve them—to come online quickly without the need for extensive study or network upgrades.

The absence of standardized, transparent procedures creates opportunities for undue discrimination in queue processing and transmission upgrade cost assignment as public utilities negotiate individual contracts with loads.

Eolian has experienced multiple cases where a transmission provider's refusal to perform an integrated study of load and generation has caused duplicative upgrade requirements that could have been avoided if load and generation had been considered together, which has impacted the business viability of both load and generation.

failure to truly enable flexible loads and provide them a means to internalize the benefits they provide to the system could otherwise result in the worst of all worlds: shifting large load interconnection to a standardized federal process that slows down progress by disrupting the potential for state innovation and failing to reduce the monumental cost of transmission expansion and capacity growth otherwise necessary to integrate large loads into the transmission system.
The Commission should build upon its experience in the generator interconnection process by further reducing barriers to use of the option to build.

bins.name: Enable and reward load flexibility
bins.desc: The core of Eolian's filing is that enforceable flexibility commitments from curtailable loads and dispatchable hybrid facilities should be recognized in the interconnection process through queue priority and streamlined study. Without this, rising upgrade costs and resource adequacy burdens will persist. Permanent non-firm service pathways are essential to make flexibility commercially viable.
quotes: Flexibility commitments from loads and hybrid resources can help to protect existing customers by avoiding the need for transmission system upgrades and allowing large loads to reduce the resource adequacy impacts of their interconnection and allow large loads—and the resources needed to serve them—to come online quickly without the need for extensive study or network upgrades.

recognizing enforceable commitments from large load customers and hybrid facilities to curtail and dispatch when called upon, providing transmission service and balancing authority operations protocols that match these commitments, and appropriately rewarding these commitments with preferred treatment in the interconnection process.

the Commission should direct transmission providers to provide for a FERC-jurisdictional load interconnection process that grants queue priority to loads that make commitments to curtail, and hybrid facilities that make commitments to dispatch and curtail, such that (a) the load or hybrid resource reduces the system's need for capacity or Network Resources, and/or (b) avoids the need for network upgrades.

the Commission should direct transmission providers to offer non-firm or conditional firm transmission service to hybrid resources that allow them to inject and withdraw up to their full potential, subject to specified system conditions and their flexibility commitments.
Without access to non-firm or conditional firm service, much of the economic value of the hybrid resource model will be lost.

failure to truly enable flexible loads and provide them a means to internalize the benefits they provide to the system could otherwise result in the worst of all worlds: shifting large load interconnection to a standardized federal process that slows down progress by disrupting the potential for state innovation and failing to reduce the monumental cost of transmission expansion and capacity growth otherwise necessary to integrate large loads into the transmission system.
the Commission should also require that transmission providers allow a pathway to permanent non-firm operation that does not require a subsequent service conversion.

bins.name: Co-location and proximate generation
bins.desc: Eolian supports allowing hybrid resources that are not strictly co-located but are electrically proximate to be studied together, and urges FERC to define hybrid facilities broadly enough to include storage and configurations across different ownership and meter arrangements.
quotes: the Commission should direct transmission providers to offer non-firm or conditional firm transmission service to hybrid resources that allow them to inject and withdraw up to their full potential, subject to specified system conditions and their flexibility commitments.

bins.name: Generation near load reduces upgrades
bins.desc: Eolian argues from engineering principles and direct experience that generation close to load requires less transmission infrastructure. Existing rules block integrated study of planned loads and nearby generation, causing duplicative upgrade requirements that harm both load and generation project viability.
quotes: Eolian has experienced multiple cases where a transmission provider's refusal to perform an integrated study of load and generation has caused duplicative upgrade requirements that could have been avoided if load and generation had been considered together, which has impacted the business viability of both load and generation.

bins.name: Clear jurisdictional transition needed
bins.desc: Eolian urges FERC to announce a clear transition rule in or before the NOPR that specifies how in-progress state-jurisdictional interconnection processes will be handled. It supports cautious entry into new jurisdictional areas and warns that federal intervention without adequate transition planning or genuine flexibility reform risks making things worse rather than better.
quotes: the Commission should state as clearly as possible in any forthcoming NOPR (or before) that it intends to provide for a transition rule and how it will propose to handle large load interconnections that have begun but not completed an existing state-jurisdictional process at the time the relevant tariff provisions go into effect.
Eolian supports the Commission treading carefully into new jurisdictional areas that have traditionally been regulated by states

failure to truly enable flexible loads and provide them a means to internalize the benefits they provide to the system could otherwise result in the worst of all worlds: shifting large load interconnection to a standardized federal process that slows down progress by disrupting the potential for state innovation and failing to reduce the monumental cost of transmission expansion and capacity growth otherwise necessary to integrate large loads into the transmission system.
